questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $$y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - x} \over {1 + x}}} } \right)$$, then $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$ is :
questions.solution:
Given, $$y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - x} \over {1 + x}}} } \right)$$

Let $$x = \cos 2\theta \Rightarrow y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over {1 + \cos 2\theta }}} } \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \sin \left( {2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {2{{\cos }^2}\theta }}} } \right) \Rightarrow y = \sin [2{\tan ^{ - 1}}(\tan \theta )]$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \sin 2\theta \Rightarrow y = \sqrt {1 - {{\cos }^2}2\theta } \Rightarrow y = \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} $$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}( - 2x) = - {x \over {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$y$$ be an implicit function of $$x$$ defined by $${x^{2x}} - 2{x^x}\cot \,y - 1 = 0$$. Then $$y'(1)$$ equals
questions.solution:
$${x^{2x}} - 2{x^x}\,\cot \,y - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2\,\cot \,y = {x^x} - {x^{ - x}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2\,\cot \,y\, = u - {1 \over u}$$

where $$u = {x^x}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $$x,$$

we get $$ \Rightarrow - 2\cos e{c^2}y{{dy} \over {dx}}$$

$$ = \left( {1 + {1 \over {{u^2}}}} \right){{du} \over {dx}}$$

where $$u = {x^x} \Rightarrow \log \,u = x\,\log \,x$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over u}{{du} \over {dx}} = 1 + \log \,x$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{du} \over {dx}} = {x^x}\left( {1 + \log \,x} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ We get $$ - 2\cos e{c^2}y{{dy} \over {dx}}$$

$$ = \left( {1 + {x^{ - 2x}}} \right){x^x}\left( {1 + \log \,x} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {{\left( {{x^x} + {x^{ - x}}} \right)\left( {1 + \log x} \right)} \over { - 2\left( {1 + {{\cot }^2}y} \right)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$

Now when

$$x=1,$$ $${x^{2x}} - 2{x^x}\,\cot \,y - 1 = 0,$$

gives $$1 - 2\,\cot y - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \,\,\cot y\, = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ From equation $$(i),$$ at $$x=1$$

and $$\cot \,y = 0,$$ we get

$$y'\left( 1 \right) = {{\left( {1 + 1} \right)\left( {1 + 0} \right)} \over { - 2\left( {1 + 0} \right)}} = - 1$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If    f(x) = sin<sup>-1</sup> $$\left( {{{2 \times {3^x}} \over {1 + {9^x}}}} \right),$$ then f'$$\left( { - {1 \over 2}} \right)$$ equals :
questions.solution:
Since f(x) = sin$$\left( {{{2 \times {3^x}} \over {1 + {9^x}}}} \right)$$

Suppose 3x = tan t

$$ \Rightarrow $$   f(x) = sin$$-$$1 $$\left( {{{2\tan t} \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}t}}} \right)$$ = sin$$-$$1 (sin2t) = 2t

                                         = 2tan$$-$$1 (3x)

So, f'(x) = $${2 \over {1 + {{\left( {{3^x}} \right)}^2}}} \times {3^x}.{\log _e}3$$

$$ \therefore $$   f '$$\left( { - {1 \over 2}} \right)$$ = $${2 \over {1 + {{\left( {{3^{ - {1 \over 2}}}} \right)}^2}}} \times {3^{ - {1 \over 2}}}$$ . loge 3

=  $${1 \over 2} \times \sqrt 3 \times {\log _e}3$$   =  $$\sqrt 3 \times {\log _e}\sqrt 3 $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a thrice differentiable odd function satisfying $f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0, f^{\prime\prime}(x)=f(x), f(0)=0, f^{\prime}(0)=3$. Then $9 f\left(\log _e 3\right)$ is equal to __________ .
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} &f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0, f^{\prime \prime}(x)=f(x)\\ &\text { Second order differential equation } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=A e^x+B e^{-x} \\ & f(0)=0 \Rightarrow A=-B \\ & \Rightarrow f(x)=A\left(e^x-e^{-x}\right) \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=A e^x+A e^{-x}=A\left(e^x+e^{-x}\right) \\ & f^{\prime}(0)=3=A\left(e^0+e^{-0}\right)=2 A \Rightarrow A=\frac{3}{2} \\ & f(x)=\frac{3}{2}\left(e^x-e^{-x}\right) \\ & \text { If }(\ln 3)=\frac{27}{2}\left(e^{\ln 3}-e^{-\ln 3}\right)=\frac{27}{2}\left(3-\frac{1}{3}\right)=\frac{27}{2} \cdot \frac{8}{3} \\ & =36 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If  xlog<sub>e</sub>(log<sub>e</sub>x) $$-$$ x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 4(y > 0),  then $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$ at x = e is equal to :
questions.solution:
Differentiating with respect to x,

$$x.{1 \over {\ell nx}}.{1 \over x} + \ell n(\ell nx) - 2x + 2y.{{dy} \over {dx}} = 0$$

at   $$x = e$$  we get

$$1 - 2e + 2y{{dy} \over {dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {{2e - 1} \over {2y}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {{2e - 1} \over {2\sqrt {4 + {e^2}} }}\,\,$$

as   $$y(e) = \sqrt {4 + {e^2}} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If  $${x^m}.{y^n} = {\left( {x + y} \right)^{m + n}},$$ then $${{{dy} \over {dx}}}$$ is
questions.solution:
$${x^m}.{y^n} = {\left( {x + y} \right)^{m + n}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow m\ln x + n\ln y = \left( {m + n} \right)\ln \left( {x + y} \right)$$

Differentiating both sides.

$$\therefore$$ $${m \over x} + {n \over y}{{dy} \over {dx}} = {{m + n} \over {x + y}}\left( {1 + {{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left( {{m \over x} - {{m + n} \over {x + y}}} \right) = \left( {{{m + n} \over {x + y}} - {n \over y}} \right){{dy} \over {dx}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{my - nx} \over {x\left( {x + y} \right)}} = \left( {{{my - nx} \over {y\left( {x + y} \right)}}} \right){{dy} \over {dx}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {y \over x}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The derivative of y with respect to x
options: depends on q only
depends on both p and qc
is independent of both p and q
questions.solution:
Differentiate by taking logarithms of both sides:

$ (p+q)\ln(x+y)=p\ln x+q\ln y. $

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$ (and writing $y’=\tfrac{dy}{dx}$) gives

$ (p+q)\frac{1+y'}{x+y} =\frac{p}{x}+\frac{q\,y'}{y}. $

Bring the $y'$-terms to one side:

$ (p+q)\frac{y'}{x+y}-\frac{q\,y'}{y} =\frac{p}{x}-\frac{p+q}{x+y}. $

Factor out $y'$:

$ y'\Bigl(\,(p+q)\frac{1}{x+y}-q\frac{1}{y}\Bigr) =\;p\frac{1}{x}-(p+q)\frac{1}{x+y}. $

Hence

$ y' =\frac{\displaystyle p\frac1x-(p+q)\frac1{x+y}} {\displaystyle (p+q)\frac1{x+y}-q\frac1y}\,, $

which manifestly involves both $p$ and $q$.

Answer: Option C.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let f and g be twice differentiable even functions on ($$-$$2, 2) such that $$f\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right) = 0$$, $$f\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right) = 0$$, $$f(1) = 1$$ and $$g\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right) = 0$$, $$g(1) = 2$$. Then, the minimum number of solutions of $$f(x)g''(x) + f'(x)g'(x) = 0$$ in $$( - 2,2)$$ is equal to ________.
questions.solution:
Let $h(x)=f(x) \cdot g^{\prime}(x)$

As $f(x)$ is even $f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)=0$

$\Rightarrow f\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=f\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)=0$

and $g(x)$ is even $\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)$ is odd

and $g(1)=2$ ensures one root of $g^{\prime}(x)$ is 0 .

So, $h(x)=f(x) \cdot g^{\prime}(x)$ has minimum five zeroes

$\therefore h^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(x) \cdot g^{\prime}(x)+f(x) \cdot g^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$,

has minimum 4 zeroes

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let f be a polynomial function such that

f (3x) = f ' (x) . f '' (x), for all x $$ \in $$ R. Then :

questions.subject: Mathematics
options: there exists $$\widehat x \in [0,3]$$ such that $$f'(\widehat x) < g'(\widehat x)$$
there exist $$0 < {x_1} < {x_2} < 3$$ such that $$f(x) < g(x),\forall x \in ({x_1},{x_2})$$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f^{\prime}(x)=2+\frac{1}{1+x^2}, g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} \\\\
& f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2}<0 \\\\
& g^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2+1\right)^{-3 / 2} \cdot 2 x<0 \\\\
& \left.f^{\prime}(x)\right|_{\min }=f^{\prime}(3)=2+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{21}{10} \\\\
& \left.g^{\prime}(x)\right|_{\max }=g^{\prime}(0)=1 \\\\
& \left.f^{\prime}(x)\right|_{\max }=f(3)=2+\tan ^{-1} 3 \\\\
& \left.g(x)\right|_{\max }=g(3)=\ln (3+\sqrt{10})<\ln <7<2
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let $$x(t)=2 \sqrt{2} \cos t \sqrt{\sin 2 t}$$ and

$$y(t)=2 \sqrt{2} \sin t \sqrt{\sin 2 t}, t \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$.

Then $$\frac{1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}$$ at $$t=\frac{\pi}{4}$$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$x = 2\sqrt 2 \cos t\sqrt {\sin 2t} ,\,y = 2\sqrt 2 \sin t\sqrt {\sin 2t} $$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dx} \over {dt}} = {{2\sqrt 2 \cos 3t} \over {\sqrt {\sin 2t} }},\,{{dy} \over {dt}} = {{2\sqrt 2 \sin 3t} \over {\sqrt {\sin 2t} }}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = \tan 3t,\,\left( {\mathrm{at}\,t = {\pi \over 4},\,{{dy} \over {dx}} = - 1} \right)$$

and $${{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}} = 3{\sec ^2}3t\,.\,{{dt} \over {dx}} = {{3{{\sec }^2}3t\,.\,\sqrt {\sin 2t} } \over {2\sqrt 2 \cos 3t}}$$

$$\left( {\mathrm{At}\,t = {\pi \over 4},\,{{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}} = - 3} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{1 + {{\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)}^2}} \over {{{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}}}} = {2 \over { - 3}} = {{ - 2} \over 3}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If xyyx = 1, then what is $\frac{dy}{dx}$ at (1, 1) equal to?
questions.solution:
To solve the given problem, we start by differentiating the equation with respect to $ x $. The equation given is $ x^y y^x = 1 $.

First, let's take the natural logarithm of both sides to simplify the expression:

$$ \ln(x^y y^x) = \ln(1) $$

Using the properties of logarithms, we can expand this as:

$$ \ln(x^y) + \ln(y^x) = 0 $$

Breaking it down further using the property $ \ln(a^b) = b \ln(a) $, we get:

$$ y \ln(x) + x \ln(y) = 0 $$

Now, we'll differentiate this equation implicitly with respect to $ x $. Remember that both $ y $ and $ x $ are functions of $ x $.

$$ \frac{d}{dx} \left( y \ln(x) + x \ln(y) \right) = \frac{d}{dx} (0) $$

Applying product rule and chain rule, we get:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} \ln(x) + y \cdot \frac{1}{x} + \ln(y) + x \cdot \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $$

Simplifying the terms, we have:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} \ln(x) + \frac{y}{x} + \ln(y) + x \cdot \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $$

We need to find $ \frac{dy}{dx} $ at the point $ (1, 1) $. At this point, $ x = 1 $ and $ y = 1 $. Substitute these values in:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} \ln(1) + \frac{1}{1} + \ln(1) + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $$

Since $ \ln(1) = 0 $, the equation simplifies to:

$$ 0 + 1 + 0 + \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $$

Thus, we find:

$$ \frac{dy}{dx} = -1 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: -1.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If  $$y = \sec \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right),$$ then $${{{dy} \over {dx}}}$$  at $$x=1$$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Let $$y = \sec \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)$$

and $${\tan ^{ - 1}}\,\,x = \theta .$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = \tan \theta $$



Thus, we have $$y = \sec \,\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \sqrt {1 + {x^2}} $$

$$\left( {\,\,} \right.$$ As $$\,\,\,\,\,\,{\sec ^2}\theta = 1 + {\tan ^2}\theta $$ $$\left. {\,\,} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {2\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} }}.2x$$

At $$x = 1,\,\,{{dy} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}.$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let f(x) be a function such that f'(x) = g(x) and f''(x) = −f(x). Let h(x) = {f(x)}2 + {g(x)}2. Then consider the following statements :

1. h'(3) = 0

2. h(1) = h(2)

Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
questions.solution:
Given:

$$f'(x) = g(x)$$

$$f''(x) = -f(x)$$

We need to find out more about the function $$h(x) = {f(x)}^2 + {g(x)}^2$$.

First, let's find the derivative of $$h(x)$$ with respect to $$x$$:

$$h'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}[{f(x)}^2 + {g(x)}^2]$$

Using the chain rule, we get:

$$h'(x) = 2f(x)f'(x) + 2g(x)g'(x)$$

Since we know that $$f'(x) = g(x)$$, we substitute this into the equation:

$$h'(x) = 2f(x)g(x) + 2g(x)g'(x)$$

Next, we need to find the expression for $$g'(x)$$. Given $$f''(x) = -f(x)$$ and using the fact that $$f''(x) = g'(x)$$, we have:

Thus:

$$g'(x) = -f(x)$$

Substituting $$g'(x) = -f(x)$$ back into our equation for $$h'(x)$$, we get:

$$h'(x) = 2f(x)g(x) + 2g(x)(-f(x))$$

$$h'(x) = 2f(x)g(x) - 2f(x)g(x)$$

$$h'(x) = 0$$

Hence, $$h'(x) = 0$$ for all $$x$$, including $$x = 3$$. So, statement 1 is correct.

Now let's consider statement 2, which says $$h(1) = h(2)$$.

Since $$h'(x) = 0$$ for all $$x$$, it implies that $$h(x)$$ is a constant function. Therefore, $$h(x)$$ has the same value for all $$x$$. So, $$h(1) = h(2)$$ must be true as well.

Therefore, statement 2 is also correct.

Thus, both statements are correct. The correct answer is:

Option C: Both 1 and 2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If y2 + loge (cos2x) = y,
$$x \in \left( { - {\pi \over 2},{\pi \over 2}} \right)$$, then :
questions.solution:
Given y2 + loge (cos2x) = y .....(1)

Put x = 0, we get

y2 + loge (1) = y

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y2 = y

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y = 0, 1

Differentiating (1) we get

2yy' + $${1 \over {\cos x}}\left( { - \sin x} \right)$$ = y'

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2yy' - 2tanx = y' ....(2)

From (2) when x = 0, y = 0 then y'(0) = 0

From (2) when x = 0, y = 1 then

2y' = y'

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y'(0) = 0

Again differentiating (2) we get

2(y')2 + 2yy'' – 2sec2x = y''

from (2) when x = 0, y = 0, y’(0) = 0 then

y”(0) = -2

Also from (2) when x = 0, y = 1, y’(0) = 0 then

y”(0) = 2

$$ \therefore $$ |y''(0)| = 2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a twice differentiable function such that $(\sin x \cos y)(f(2 x+2 y)-f(2 x-2 y))=(\cos x \sin y)(f(2 x+2 y)+f(2 x-2 y))$, for all $x, y \in \mathbf{R}$. If $f^{\prime}(0)=\frac{1}{2}$, then the value of $24 f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{3}\right)$ is :
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & \sin (x-y) f(2 x+2 y)=f(2 x-2 y) \sin (x+y) \\ & \frac{f(2 x+2 y)}{\sin (x+y)}=\frac{f(2 x-2 y)}{\sin (x-y)}=k(\text { say }) \\ & f(2 x+2 y)=k \sin (x+y) \\ & f(2 x)=5 \sin x \quad(\because y=0) \\ & f(x)=k \sin \frac{x}{2} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{k}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2} \\ & f^{\prime}(0)=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow k=1 \\ & f(x)=\sin \frac{x}{2} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2} \\ & f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{4} \sin \frac{x}{2} \\ & 24 f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{5 \pi}{3}\right)=-3 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is $f''(x)$ equal to?
questions.solution:
To find the second derivative of the function $ f(x) $, we need to determine $ f(x) $ first using the given information.

Given:

1. $ g(x) = x - \frac{1}{x} $

2. $ f \circ g(x) = x^3 - \frac{1}{x^3} $

This means $ f(g(x)) = x^3 - \frac{1}{x^3} $. So, we need to express $ f(u) $ where $ u = g(x) = x - \frac{1}{x} $.

Let $ u = x - \frac{1}{x} $. This converts to:

$$ f(u) = x^3 - \frac{1}{x^3} $$

We need to express $ x $ and $ \frac{1}{x} $ in terms of $ u $. If we cube $ u $, we get:

$$ u^3 = \left( x - \frac{1}{x} \right)^3 $$

Expanding this, we get:

$$ u^3 = x^3 - 3x \cdot \frac{1}{x} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{x^2} \cdot x - \frac{1}{x^3} $$

$$ u^3 = x^3 - 3\left( x - \frac{1}{x} \right) - \frac{1}{x^3} $$

Since $ u = x - \frac{1}{x} $, we substitute $ u $ back in:

$$ u^3 = x^3 - 3u - \frac{1}{x^3} $$

From this, we get:

$$ x^3 - \frac{1}{x^3} = u^3 + 3u $$

Thus, we can write:

$$ f(u) = u^3 + 3u $$

Now, we need to find the second derivative of $ f(x) = x^3 + 3x $.

First derivative $ f'(x) $ is:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^3 + 3x) = 3x^2 + 3 $$

Second derivative $ f''(x) $ is:

$$ f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 + 3) = 6x $$

So, the correct option is:

Option D

6x

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $$2 x^{y}+3 y^{x}=20$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ at $$(2,2)$$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Given, $2 x^y+3 y^x=20$ ..........(i)

Let $u=x^y$

On taking log both sides, we get

$\log u=y \log x$

On differentiating both sides with respect to $x$, we get

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} & \frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x} =y \frac{1}{x}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x} =u\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}\right) \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x} =x^y\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}\right) .........(ii) \end{array} $$

Also, let $v=y^x$

On taking log both sides, we get

$\log v=x \log y$

On differentiating both sides, we get

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} & \frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x} =x \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y \cdot 1 \\\\ &\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x} =v\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right) \\\\ &\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x} =y^x\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right) ..........(iii) \end{array} $$

Now, from Equation (i), $2 u+3 v=20$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2 \frac{d u}{d x}+3 \frac{d v}{d x}=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 2 x^y\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}\right)+3 y^x\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right)=0 \text { [Using Eqs. (ii) and (iii)] } \end{aligned} $$

On putting $x=2$ and $y=2$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2(4)\left(1+\log 2 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)+3(4)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}+\log 2\right)=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}(8 \log 2+12)+(8+12 \log 2)=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{2+3 \log 2}{3+2 \log 2}\right) \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{2+\log 8}{3+\log 4}\right) \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let $$f:\left[ {0,2} \right] \to R$$ be a function which is continuous on $$\left[ {0,2} \right]$$ and is differentiable on $$(0,2)$$ with $$f(0)=1$$. Let
$$F\left( x \right) = \int\limits_0^{{x^2}} {f\left( {\sqrt t } \right)dt} $$ for $$x \in \left[ {0,2} \right]$$. If $$F'\left( x \right) = f'\left( x \right)$$ for all $$x \in \left[ {0,2} \right]$$, then $$F(2)$$ equals
questions.solution:
Given, $$\mathrm{F}^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(x)\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

And $$\quad \mathrm{F}(x)=\int_0^{x^2} f(\sqrt{t}) d t$$

Differentiate the equation (ii) w.r.t. $$x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{F}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{d\left(x^2\right)}{d x} \cdot f\left(\sqrt{x^2}\right)-\frac{d(0)}{d x} \cdot f(\sqrt{0}) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=2 x \cdot f(x)-0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d f(x)}{f(x)}=2 x d x \\ & \Rightarrow \int \frac{d f(x)}{f(x)}=\int 2 x d x \\ & \Rightarrow \ln f(x)=2 \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}+\mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \ln f(x)=x^2+\mathrm{C} \quad \text{... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Given, $$f(0)=1$$

$$\begin{array}{llrl} & \therefore & \ln (1) & =0^2+C \\ & \Rightarrow & 0 & =0+C \\ \Rightarrow & & C=0 \end{array}$$

Put $$c=0$$ in the equation (iii)

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \ln f(x)=x^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad f(x) & =e^{x^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad f(\sqrt{t}) & =e^t \end{array}$$

Put $$f(\sqrt{t})=e^t$$ in the equation (ii)

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \mathrm{F}(x)=\int_0^{x^2} e^t \cdot d t \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{F}(x)=\left[e^t\right]_0^{x^2} \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{F}(x)=e^{x^2}-1 \end{array}$$

Put $$\quad x=2$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{F}(2)=e^4-1$$

Hint:

(i) If $$y=\int_{\phi(x)}^{\psi(x)} f(t) d t$$, then $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d \psi(x)}{d x} \cdot f(\psi(x))-\frac{d \phi(x)}{d x} \cdot f(\phi(x))$$

(ii) $$\int \frac{f^{\prime}(x) d x}{f(x)}=\ln (f(x))+c$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements :
1. $f''(e) = \frac{1}{e}$
2. $f(x)$ attains local minimum value at $x = e$
3. A local minimum value of $f(x)$ is $e$
Which of the statements given above are correct ?
questions.solution:
Given the function

$$ f(x) = \frac{x}{\ln x} \; \text{with} \; (x > 1), $$

let's analyze the provided statements step by step.

First Derivative

First, compute the first derivative $ f'(x) $:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \left( \frac{x}{\ln x} \right). $$

Using the quotient rule:

$$ f'(x) = \frac{ (\ln x) \cdot (1) - x \cdot \frac{1}{x} }{(\ln x)^2} = \frac{ \ln x - 1}{(\ln x)^2}. $$

Second Derivative

Next, compute the second derivative $ f''(x) $:

$$ f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \left( \frac{\ln x - 1}{(\ln x)^2} \right). $$

Applying the quotient rule again:

$ f''(x) = \frac{ (\ln x)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{x} - (\ln x - 1) \cdot 2 (\ln x) \cdot \frac{1}{x} }{ (\ln x)^4 } = \frac{ (\ln x) - 2 (\ln x - 1) }{ x (\ln x)^3 } = \frac{ \ln x - 2 \ln x + 2 }{ x (\ln x)^3 } = \frac{ 2 - \ln x }{ x (\ln x)^3 }. $

Evaluating $ f''(x) $ at $ x = e $:

$$ f''(e) = \frac{ 2 - \ln e }{ e (\ln e)^3 } = \frac{ 2 - 1 }{ e (1)^3 } = \frac{1}{e}. $$

So, statement 1 is correct.

Local Minimum Value

To find where $ f(x) $ attains a local minimum, consider the critical points. Set $ f'(x) = 0 $:

$$ \frac{ \ln x - 1}{ (\ln x)^2 } = 0 \Rightarrow \ln x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \ln x = 1 \Rightarrow x = e. $$

To determine the nature of this critical point, check the sign of $ f''(x) $ around $ x = e $. Since $ f''(e) = \frac{1}{e} > 0 $, $ x = e $ is a local minimum.

The function $ f(x) $ attains a local minimum value at $ x = e $.

Lastly, compute $ f(e) $:

$$ f(e) = \frac{e}{\ln e} = \frac{e}{1} = e. $$

Therefore, the local minimum value of $ f(x) $ is $ e $.

Conclusion

Statements 1, 2, and 3 are correct. Hence, the correct option is:

Option D

$ \text{Statement 1: } f''(e) = \frac{1}{e} \text{ is correct.} $

$ \text{Statement 2: } f(x) \text{ attains a local minimum value at } x = e \text{ is correct.} $

$ \text{Statement 3: } \text{A local minimum value of } f(x) \text{ is } e \text{ is correct.} $

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $f(x)=x^3+x^2 f^{\prime}(1)+x f^{\prime \prime}(2)+f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3), x \in \mathbf{R}$. Then $f^{\prime}(10)$ is equal to ____________.
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=x^3+x^2 \cdot f^{\prime}(1)+x \cdot f^{\prime \prime}(2)+f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3) \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2+2 x f^{\prime}(1)+f^{\prime \prime}(2) \\ & f^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 x+2 f^{\prime}(1) \\ & f^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=6 \\ & f^{\prime}(1)=-5, f^{\prime \prime}(2)=2, f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3)=6 \\ & f(x)=x^3+x^2 \cdot(-5)+x \cdot(2)+6 \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-10 x+2 \\ & f^{\prime}(10)=300-100+2=202 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is $\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$ at x = 0 equal to?
questions.solution:
Given

$$ y=\frac{x\sin^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}+\ln\sqrt{1-x^2} $$

We need to find

$$ \left.\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right|_{x=0} $$

Let us simplify first.

Since

$$ \ln\sqrt{1-x^2}=\frac12\ln(1-x^2) $$

so,

$$ y=x\sin^{-1}x\,(1-x^2)^{-1/2}+\frac12\ln(1-x^2) $$

To find $y''(0)$ easily, we use the Maclaurin expansion near $x=0$.

We know:

$$ \sin^{-1}x=x+\frac{x^3}{6}+\cdots $$

and

$$ (1-x^2)^{-1/2}=1+\frac{x^2}{2}+\cdots $$

So,

$$ x\sin^{-1}x=x\left(x+\frac{x^3}{6}+\cdots\right) = x^2+\frac{x^4}{6}+\cdots $$

Now multiply by $(1-x^2)^{-1/2}$:

$$ \frac{x\sin^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} =\left(x^2+\frac{x^4}{6}+\cdots\right)\left(1+\frac{x^2}{2}+\cdots\right) $$

The coefficient of $x^2$ is just $1$, so

$$ \frac{x\sin^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}=x^2+\cdots $$

Also,

$$ \frac12\ln(1-x^2) =\frac12\left(-x^2-\frac{x^4}{2}-\cdots\right) =-\frac{x^2}{2}+\cdots $$

Therefore,

$$ y=x^2-\frac{x^2}{2}+\cdots =\frac{x^2}{2}+\cdots $$

Now compare with the Maclaurin form

$$ y=y(0)+y'(0)x+\frac{y''(0)}{2}x^2+\cdots $$

Coefficient of $x^2$ is $\frac12$, so

$$ \frac{y''(0)}{2}=\frac12 $$

Hence,

$$ y''(0)=1 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{1} $$

Option 3.
